title: What is the Difference Between Subsidized and Unsubsidized Student Loans?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You filed the FAFSA. You received your financial aid award letter. It includes two types of federal student loans: Direct Subsidized Loans and Direct Unsubsidized Loans. The names are almost identical. The difference sounds like bureaucratic fine print. It is not. The difference affects how much you owe when you graduate, and it can add thousands of dollars to your debt over the life of the loan. Here is what each type means, how they work, and which one you should accept first.

The Core Difference

The difference between subsidized and unsubsidized loans is who pays the interest while you are in school. With a subsidized loan, the federal government pays the interest while you are enrolled at least half-time, during your six-month grace period after graduation, and during any periods of deferment. You borrow a dollar. You owe a dollar when repayment begins. With an unsubsidized loan, interest begins accruing the day the loan is disbursed. You borrow a dollar. Interest accumulates while you are in class, while you are studying for exams, and while you are sleeping. When repayment begins, you owe that dollar plus all the interest that has been piling up for years.

This difference compounds dramatically over time. A student who borrows five thousand dollars in unsubsidized loans their first year, at a six percent interest rate, will owe approximately six thousand dollars by the time repayment begins four years later. The same five thousand dollars borrowed as a subsidized loan will still be five thousand dollars when repayment begins. The interest did not disappear. The government paid it on your behalf. That is the subsidy.

Eligibility Requirements

Subsidized loans are awarded based on financial need. The FAFSA determines whether you qualify. If your family's expected contribution is low enough, you will be offered subsidized loans as part of your financial aid package. There are annual limits on how much you can borrow in subsidized loans. For first-year students, the maximum is typically three thousand five hundred dollars. The limit increases in subsequent years.

Unsubsidized loans are available to all eligible students regardless of financial need. You do not need to demonstrate need to qualify. The annual limits are higher, but your total aid package, including all loans, grants, and scholarships, cannot exceed the cost of attendance at your school.

Graduate students are not eligible for subsidized loans. This is a recent change. Graduate students can still borrow unsubsidized loans and PLUS loans, but the days of interest-free borrowing during graduate school are over.

How Interest Capitalization Works

With unsubsidized loans, the interest that accrues while you are in school does not just sit there passively. At the end of your grace period, the accrued interest capitalizes. This means it is added to your principal balance. You are now paying interest on your interest. This is a painful mechanism that increases the total cost of your loan significantly.

You can prevent capitalization by making interest payments while you are still in school. Even small payments, twenty or thirty dollars a month, prevent interest from piling up and capitalizing. Most students do not do this because they do not have the cash flow during school, but it is worth considering if you have income from a part-time job. Paying the interest as it accrues on unsubsidized loans saves you from paying interest on that interest later.

The Order of Operations

When you receive your financial aid award, accept loans in this order. First, accept all subsidized loans you are offered. This is the cheapest money you will ever borrow. The government pays the interest while you are in school. There is no downside relative to other loans.

Second, only after you have accepted all subsidized loans, consider unsubsidized loans if you still need additional funds to cover your educational expenses. Unsubsidized loans are still federal loans, which means they come with federal borrower protections. Income-driven repayment plans. Deferment and forbearance options. Loan forgiveness programs. These protections make federal unsubsidized loans preferable to private student loans, even though both accrue interest during school.

Third, only after you have exhausted your federal loan options, consider private student loans as a last resort. Private loans lack the borrower protections of federal loans. They often have higher and variable interest rates. They are not eligible for federal forgiveness programs. If you must borrow privately, shop around. Compare rates from multiple lenders. Have a cosigner with good credit if possible. A cosigner can significantly reduce your interest rate.

The Big Picture

Subsidized loans are the best student loan product available. Borrow as much as you are offered. Unsubsidized federal loans are second best because of their borrower protections. Borrow only what you need. Private loans are a distant third. Borrow as little as possible, and only after exhausting federal options.

Before you borrow anything, calculate your total debt at graduation and your estimated monthly payment. Federal student loan repayment calculators are available online. Know what you are agreeing to before you sign. A loan accepted today is a monthly payment you will make for ten to twenty-five years. Borrow with your eyes open.

The Bottom Line

Subsidized loans do not accrue interest while you are in school. Unsubsidized loans do. This difference can add thousands of dollars to your debt over the life of the loan. Accept subsidized loans first. Accept unsubsidized loans next if you need them. Consider private loans only as a last resort. Borrow the minimum you need, and know what your monthly payment will be before you sign. Student loans are an investment in your future earning potential. Like any investment, the terms matter. Read them. Understand them. Make them work for you.
